mnemonics.title: Pipe Creation Remembering
mnemonics.content: R-W: Remember 'Read comes first (0), Write comes second (1)' - fd[0] for reading, fd[1] for writing

mnemonics.title: Pipe Close Convention
mnemonics.content: Child closes read end (fd[0]) if sending data to parent; Parent closes write end (fd[1]) after forking

acronyms.usage: Named pipe type that maintains order of data
Mechanisms like pipes, sockets, message queues for process communication
Maximum bytes guaranteed to be written atomically (typically 4096)
Returned by read() when all write ends are closed

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pipe as a physical tube - water (data) flows in one direction from one end to the other. You write at one end and read from the other.
Imagine fd[0] as the mouth (input) and fd[1] as the tail (output) - 0 comes before 1, just as reading comes before writing in the data flow concept.
A pipe is like a conversation where parent holds one end of telephone and child holds the other - they need to be connected (forked) first.

analogies.analogy: Like a walkie-talkie: two people (related processes) can talk, but only one direction at a time, and they must be within range (related).
Like a mailbox at a street corner: anyone (any process) can drop letters (write) and anyone can pick them up (read) - no relationship needed.
PIPE_BUF is like a single elevator trip - if you're within the weight limit (PIPE_BUF), you go together atomically;超过这个可能需要多趟
